Phase 10 — Bootstrap Confidence Intervals

Empirical parameter uncertainty by subject resampling

Author

Pramod BR

Published

June 28, 2026

1 Objectives

The parameter CIs reported so far are asymptotic — derived from the covariance matrix under large-sample, normal assumptions. With only 12 subjects those assumptions are questionable. This phase computes bootstrap CIs that make no such assumption, and compares the two.

2 Method — case (subject) resampling

We repeatedly draw 12 subjects with replacement from the original 12, refit the IIV model to each resampled dataset, and collect the fixed effects. The 2.5th and 97.5th percentiles of those estimates form an empirical 95% CI.

Code 
dat  <- build_nonmem_data()
fit  <- load_iiv_fit()
boot <- run_bootstrap(dat, nboot = 50)
calculating covariance matrix

50 of 50 replicates converged (100%). A convergence rate below 100% is normal — some resamples produce awkward datasets (e.g. the same subject drawn several times) — and the rate itself is a useful stability check.

3 Asymptotic vs bootstrap 95% CI

Code 
boot_summary(boot, fit) |>
  kbl(caption = "Point estimate with bootstrap median and 95% percentile interval",
      digits = 3) |>
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover", "condensed"),
                full_width = FALSE)
Point estimate with bootstrap median and 95% percentile interval
Parameter Estimate Boot median Boot 2.5% Boot 97.5%
ka 1.475 1.441 1.135 2.417
cl 2.787 2.806 2.399 3.297
v 32.010 31.981 29.248 35.285
add.sd 0.261 0.237 0.043 0.476
prop.sd 0.136 0.141 0.058 0.193

Read this against the asymptotic CIs from Phase 4. Agreement between the two reassures us the asymptotic SEs were trustworthy; a wider or skewed bootstrap interval (most likely for Ka, the least precise parameter) reveals uncertainty the formula understated.

4 Bootstrap distributions

Code 
boot_plot(boot)

5 Key takeaways

  • Parameter uncertainty was quantified empirically by subject resampling, free of the normality assumption behind asymptotic SEs.
  • The bootstrap and asymptotic CIs can be compared directly; close agreement validates the reported precision, while divergence (typically for Ka) flags small-sample fragility.
  • With n = 12 the bootstrap is itself coarse and presented as a demonstration of method — exactly how it would be framed for a small study.

Next (Phase 11): a covariate forest plot summarising the weight effect on CL and V with confidence intervals. ```
